runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 10 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Gruber and Saez (2002) - Elasticity of taxable income estimates by income group
CBO microsimulation parameters for labor supply elasticities
Immervoll et al. (2007) - EUROMOD-style tax-benefit microsimulation calibration
runs.reasoningSummary: For primary earners in the top decile, substitution elasticities in tax-benefit microsimulations are typically modest but somewhat higher than for middle-decile earners. The literature on taxable income elasticities (Gruber & Saez, Saez et al.) suggests higher-income individuals have elasticities in the 0.1–0.4 range, with compensated/substitution elasticities for primary earners tending toward 0.15–0.30. Microsimulation models like those used by CBO or TRIM3 typically calibrate primary-earner substitution elasticities for top-decile workers around 0.15–0.25. I center my estimate at 0.20, reflecting that top-decile primary earners have somewhat more responsiveness than average workers (due to more scope for income shifting, deduction timing, and some hours flexibility) but are still constrained in their labor supply responses. Uncertainty spans from about 0.05 (if the model uses very conservative behavioral parameters) to 0.45 (if it incorporates broader taxable income responses including avoidance margins).

(2007) - EUROMOD-style tax-benefit microsimulation calibration"], "reasoning_summary": "For primary earners in the top decile, substitution elasticities in tax-benefit microsimulations are typically modest but somewhat higher than for middle-decile earners. The literature on taxable income elasticities (Gruber & Saez, Saez et al.) suggests higher-income individuals have elasticities in the 0.1\u20130.4 range, with compensated/substitution elasticities for primary earners tending toward 0.15\u20130.30. Microsimulation models like those used by CBO or TRIM3 typically calibrate primary-earner substitution elasticities for top-decile workers around 0.15\u20130.25. I center my estimate at 0.20, reflecting that top-decile primary earners have somewhat more responsiveness than average workers (due to more scope for income shifting, deduction timing, and some hours flexibility) but are still constrained in their labor supply responses. Uncertainty spans from about 0.05 (if the model uses very conservative behavioral parameters) to 0.45 (if it incorporates broader taxable income responses including avoidance margins)."}

runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 10 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Gruber and Saez (2002) - Elasticity of taxable income with respect to marginal tax rates
CBO (2012) - How the Supply of Labor Responds to Changes in Fiscal Policy
Immervoll et al. (2007) - EUROMOD-style microsimulation substitution elasticities
runs.reasoningSummary: For top-decile primary earners in a U.S. tax-benefit microsimulation, the substitution elasticity of labor supply with respect to the net-of-tax wage is typically calibrated in the range of 0.1–0.3. Primary earners (typically men, full-time workers) have relatively inelastic labor supply overall, but at the top of the earnings distribution, the taxable income elasticity literature (Gruber & Saez, Saez et al.) suggests moderate responsiveness on the intensive/substitution margin, often around 0.1–0.4. Microsimulation models like TAXSIM or CBO's labor supply modules tend to use substitution elasticities for primary earners in the range of 0.1–0.3, with top earners sometimes assigned slightly higher values due to greater scope for adjusting taxable income (timing, deductions, portfolio allocation). I center my estimate at 0.20 with moderate uncertainty spanning roughly 0.05 to 0.45.

Primary earners (typically men, full-time workers) have relatively inelastic labor supply overall, but at the top of the earnings distribution, the taxable income elasticity literature (Gruber & Saez, Saez et al.) suggests moderate responsiveness on the intensive/substitution margin, often around 0.1\u20130.4. runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 10 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Gruber and Saez (2002) - Elasticity of taxable income estimates by income group
CBO microsimulation assumptions for labor supply elasticities
Immervoll et al. (2007) - EUROMOD-style tax-benefit microsimulation elasticity calibrations
runs.reasoningSummary: For top-decile primary earners, the substitution elasticity used in U.S. tax-benefit microsimulations typically draws on taxable income elasticity literature. Primary earners (typically male household heads) have relatively inelastic labor supply on the intensive margin, with compensated/substitution elasticities commonly in the 0.1–0.3 range. However, at the top of the earnings distribution, behavioral responses to marginal tax rate changes can be somewhat larger due to avoidance/timing channels, pushing estimates upward. Gruber and Saez (2002) found taxable income elasticities around 0.2–0.4 for higher earners, though pure hours-based substitution elasticities are lower. Microsimulation models like those used by CBO or TRIM typically calibrate primary-earner substitution elasticities in the 0.1–0.3 range for top earners. I center my estimate at 0.20 with moderate uncertainty reflecting variation across model specifications and the specific definition of the bounded relative wage change.

runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 10 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Gruber and Saez (2002) - Elasticity of taxable income estimates by income group
Congressional Budget Office (CBO) microsimulation substitution elasticity assumptions
Saez, Slemrod, and Giertz (2012) - Review of elasticity of taxable income literature
runs.reasoningSummary: For top-decile primary earners in a U.S. tax-benefit microsimulation, the substitution elasticity reflects how labor supply (or taxable income) responds to changes in the net-of-tax wage rate. The literature on taxable income elasticities (Gruber & Saez 2002, Saez et al. 2012) generally finds compensated elasticities in the range of 0.1–0.4 for high earners, with central estimates around 0.2–0.25. CBO and similar microsimulation models typically calibrate primary-earner substitution elasticities for high-income deciles in the 0.15–0.30 range. Top-decile earners tend to have somewhat higher elasticities than middle-decile earners due to greater scope for income shifting and adjustment on the intensive margin, but primary earners (as opposed to secondary earners) tend to have lower elasticities overall. Balancing these considerations, I center my estimate at 0.20 with moderate uncertainty spanning roughly 0.05 to 0.45.

CBO and similar microsimulation models typically calibrate primary-earner substitution elasticities for high-income deciles in the 0.15\u20130.30 range. Top-decile earners tend to have somewhat higher elasticities than middle-decile earners due to greater scope for income shifting and adjustment on the intensive margin, but primary earners (as opposed to secondary earners) tend to have lower elasticities overall. Balancing these considerations, I center my estimate at 0.20 with moderate uncertainty spanning roughly 0.05 to 0.45."}

The taxable income elasticity literature (Gruber & Saez, Saez et al.) finds compensated elasticities around 0.1–0.4 for high earners, with central estimates near 0.2–0.25. In microsimulation models (e.g., CBO's approach), primary earners generally have lower labor supply elasticities than secondary earners, and high-income primary earners tend to have moderate substitution responses. A value around 0.20 reflects the typical calibration for this group: not as high as for secondary earners or lower-income workers on the extensive margin, but meaningfully positive given that high earners can adjust hours, effort, and reported income in response to marginal wage changes. The uncertainty range (0.05–0.45) captures variation across model specifications and the literature.

In a bounded microsimulation context, the parameter is typically calibrated conservatively, centering around 0.20 for the top decile. I place my median at 0.20 with moderate uncertainty spanning roughly 0.05 to 0.45.

In a bounded microsimulation context, the parameter is typically calibrated conservatively, centering around 0.20 for the top decile. I place my median at 0.20 with moderate uncertainty spanning roughly 0.05 to 0.45."}
